examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize distances = [inf, 0, inf, inf] for nodes 1-4. Start DFS from node 2 with time 0. Step 2: From node 2, edges: to node 1 (weight 1) and to node 3 (weight 1). Explore node 1 first: new time = 0+1=1, update distances[0]=1, node 1 has no outgoing edges, return. Step 3: Back to node 2, explore node 3: new time = 0+1=1, update distances[2]=1, from node 3 edge to node 4: new time = 1+1=2, update distances[3]=2, node 4 has no edges, return. Step 4: DFS complete, distances = [1, 0, 1, 2], max=2.

Step 1: Initialize distances = [inf, 0] for nodes 1-2. Start DFS from node 2 with time 0. Step 2: Node 2 has no outgoing edges (times only has edge from 1 to 2). Step 3: DFS complete, distances = [inf, 0], max=inf so return -1.

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS O(n^n)
approaches.summary: Explore all possible paths from the source node to every other node using depth-first search without pruning, calculating delays along each path. This is impractical for large networks due to exponential time complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Network Delay Time with Brute Force DFS
ladders.desc: Find maximum delay from source to all nodes by exploring all possible paths without pruning.

explanation.prose: This brute force DFS approach solves the network delay time problem by exhaustively exploring every possible path from the source node to every other node in the network. The intuition is that by considering all paths without any optimization or pruning, we're guaranteed to find the actual shortest path distances, but at an enormous computational cost. The algorithm works by performing a depth-first traversal from the source node, following every outgoing edge at each step, and accumulating delays along each path. When we reach a node, we update its minimum delay if the current path has a smaller delay than previously recorded. Since we explore all paths, we eventually find the true minimum delay to each node. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Initialize a distances array with infinity for all nodes except source (0). 2) Perform DFS from source with current delay = 0. 3) At each node, for each neighbor, recursively explore with updated delay = current delay + edge weight. 4) If reaching a node with smaller delay than recorded, update distance and continue exploring. 5) After DFS completes, find maximum distance among all nodes (return -1 if any node unreachable). This connects to the parent problem as the complete implementation of the brute force approach.

timeComplexity.note: in worst case because at each of n nodes, we might explore up to n branches, leading to exponential growth

spaceComplexity.note: for recursion stack and distance storage

commonMistakes: not handling cycles properly (DFS should continue even if revisiting nodes since shorter paths might exist)
forgetting to update distances when finding better paths
explanation.codePattern: def networkDelayTime(times, n, k): distances = [float('inf')] * n; distances[k-1] = 0; def dfs(node, current_time): for u, v, w in times: if u == node+1: new_time = current_time + w; if new_time < distances[v-1]: distances[v-1] = new_time; dfs(v-1, new_time); dfs(k-1, 0); max_time = max(distances); return max_time if max_time < float('inf') else -1

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize distances = [inf, 0, inf, inf] for nodes 1-4. Start DFS from node 2 with time 0. Step 2: From node 2, edges: to node 1 (weight 1) and to node 3 (weight 1). Explore node 1 first: new time = 0+1=1, update distances[0]=1, node 1 has no outgoing edges, return. Step 3: Back to node 2, explore node 3: new time = 0+1=1, update distances[2]=1, from node 3 edge to node 4: new time = 1+1=2, update distances[3]=2, node 4 has no edges, return. Step 4: DFS complete, distances = [1, 0, 1, 2], max=2.

Step 1: Initialize distances = [inf, 0] for nodes 1-2. Start DFS from node 2 with time 0. Step 2: Node 2 has no outgoing edges (times only has edge from 1 to 2). Step 3: DFS complete, distances = [inf, 0], max=inf so return -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Path Exploration with Delay Accumulation
ladders.desc: Recursive DFS that explores all paths from a node while tracking accumulated delay.

explanation.prose: This DFS sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach, responsible for exploring every possible path from a given starting node. The intuition is that by recursively following all outgoing edges without any pruning or visited checks (except for delay comparisons), we guarantee exploration of the entire path space. The algorithm works by taking a current node and current delay, then for each outgoing edge from that node, it calculates the new delay to the neighbor and recursively explores from that neighbor. The key insight is that we only update the recorded distance for a node if we find a path with smaller delay, but we continue exploring even from nodes we've visited before because a different path might yield a better delay later. Step-by-step: 1) Base case: none needed as recursion stops when no outgoing edges. 2) For each edge where source equals current node: calculate new_delay = current_delay + edge_weight. 3) If new_delay < stored_distance[neighbor]: update distance and recursively call DFS on neighbor with new_delay. 4) Continue until all edges processed. Time complexity for this subroutine alone is O(E) per call in worst case, but with recursion it contributes to overall O(n^n). This feeds into L0 by being the main exploration mechanism that the full problem repeatedly invokes.
commonMistakes: using visited flags (which would prune paths prematurely)
explanation.codePattern: def dfs(node, current_time): for source, target, weight in edges: if source == node: new_time = current_time + weight; if new_time < distances[target]: distances[target] = new_time; dfs(target, new_time)

examples.input: Start node=1, current_time=0, edges=[[1,2,3],[1,3,5],[2,3,1]], distances=[inf,0,inf,inf] for nodes 1-3
examples.trace: Step 1: At node 1 with time 0. Process edge to node 2: new_time=0+3=3, distances[2]=inf so update to 3, recurse to node 2. Step 2: At node 2 with time 3. Process edge to node 3: new_time=3+1=4, distances[3]=inf so update to 4. Step 3: Back to node 1, process edge to node 3: new_time=0+5=5, distances[3]=4, 5>4 so no update. Final distances: node1=0, node2=3, node3=4.

examples.input: Start node=2, current_time=1, edges=[[2,3,2]], distances=[5,1,inf] for nodes 1-3
examples.trace: Step 1: At node 2 with time 1. Process edge to node 3: new_time=1+2=3, distances[3]=inf so update to 3. No more edges. Final distances unchanged except node3=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Exhaustive Path Exploration Without Pruning
ladders.desc: The technique of exploring all possible paths by always following every outgoing edge.

explanation.prose: This core logic represents the fundamental technique that makes the brute force approach work: the decision to never prune any exploration path, regardless of whether we've visited a node before or found seemingly worse paths. The intuition is that in graph shortest path problems, a node might be reachable via multiple paths with different costs, and a path that initially looks longer might lead to a better overall path later. By exploring every single possible sequence of edges from the source, we're guaranteed to eventually traverse the actual shortest path to each node. The algorithm works by maintaining the principle: at every node, for every outgoing edge, always explore the neighbor regardless of current state. The only condition is that if we arrive at a node with a better (smaller) accumulated delay than previously recorded, we update that node's best-known delay and continue exploring from there. This creates exponential branching because each node can be revisited multiple times via different paths. Time complexity arises from potentially exploring n^n paths. Space complexity is linear for storing current path in recursion.

connectsTo.reason: as the guiding principle behind the DFS implementation

commonMistakes: adding visited sets (which would turn this into a different algorithm)
stopping exploration when finding a path (missing potentially better ones)
explanation.codePattern: At each node: for each neighbor: explore(neighbor, current_delay + edge_weight)

examples.trace: Step 1: From node 1, two edges: to node 2 and to node 3. Step 2: Explore node 2 first: delay=1. From node 2, edge to node 3: delay=2. Step 3: Back to node 1, explore node 3: delay=3. Both paths explored fully.

examples.output: Explores infinite paths theoretically, but with delay tracking stops when no better paths
examples.trace: Step 1: From node 1 to node 2: delay=1. Step 2: From node 2 to node 1: delay=2. Step 3: From node 1 again to node 2: delay=3 (worse than existing 1, so no update). Exploration continues but no new updates.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal with Edge Iteration
ladders.desc: Navigating a graph by processing all outgoing edges from a node.

explanation.prose: This building block involves the fundamental skill of traversing a graph structure by examining and following edges from a given node. The intuition is that graphs represent connections between nodes, and traversal requires systematically checking each connection to discover reachable nodes. The algorithm works by representing the graph (typically as a list of edges), identifying all edges where the source matches the current node, and then processing each such edge to access the neighbor node. In the context of our brute force approach, this means for a given node, we find all edges where the first element equals the node identifier, extract the target node and weight, and then use that information to continue traversal. Step-by-step: 1) Input: current node ID, list of edges. 2) Filter edges where edge[0] == current node. 3) For each matching edge: extract neighbor = edge[1], weight = edge[2]. 4) Perform operation (in our case, recursive call).

connectsTo.reason: as the mechanism that enables exhaustive exploration—without this edge iteration, we couldn't follow all paths

timeComplexity.note: per node where E is total edges

explanation.codePattern: for edge in edges: if edge[0] == current_node: neighbor, weight = edge[1], edge[2]; # process

examples.trace: Step 1: Examine edge [1,2,1]: source=1 ≠ 2, skip. Step 2: Edge [2,3,2]: source=2 match, neighbor=3, weight=2. Step 3: Edge [2,4,3]: source=2 match, neighbor=4, weight=3. Step 4: Edge [3,1,1]: source=3 ≠ 2, skip. Found 2 neighbors.
Step 1: Only edge [1,2,5]: source=1 match, neighbor=2, weight=5. One neighbor.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Delay Accumulation and Comparison
ladders.desc: Adding edge weights to current delay and comparing with stored values.

explanation.prose: This basic operation involves two key mathematical/comparison tasks: accumulating delays along a path by summing edge weights, and comparing this accumulated value with previously stored values to determine if we've found a better path. The intuition is that in pathfinding problems, the total cost of a path is the sum of individual edge costs, and we need to track the minimum cost discovered so far. The algorithm works by maintaining a current_delay variable that represents the total time from source to current node along the current path. When we traverse an edge with weight w, we calculate new_delay = current_delay + w. We then compare new_delay with the best-known delay for the target node (stored in a distances array). If new_delay is smaller, we update the stored value and continue exploration; if not, we still continue exploration (in brute force approach) because future paths might yield better values. Step-by-step: 1) Start with current_delay. 2) Given edge weight w: new_delay = current_delay + w. 3) Retrieve stored_delay for target node. 4) If new_delay < stored_delay: update stored_delay = new_delay. 5) Use new_delay for further traversal.

connectsTo.reason: as the computation performed during edge iteration

explanation.codePattern: new_time = current_time + weight; if new_time < distances[neighbor]: distances[neighbor] = new_time

examples.trace: Step 1: Calculate new_delay = 5 + 3 = 8. Step 2: Compare 8 < 10? Yes. Step 3: Update stored_delay = 8.
Step 1: Calculate new_delay = 7 + 2 = 9. Step 2: Compare 9 < 5? No. Step 3: Keep stored_delay = 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Representing Graphs with Edge Lists
ladders.desc: Understanding how graphs are represented using lists of connections.

explanation.prose: This foundational concept involves understanding how graphs—networks of nodes connected by edges—can be represented in code using simple data structures. The intuition is that we need a way to store which nodes are connected and what the connection weights are. The most straightforward representation is an edge list: a list of tuples where each tuple contains three elements: (source_node, target_node, weight). For example, [(1,2,5), (2,3,3)] means there's an edge from node 1 to node 2 with weight 5, and from node 2 to node 3 with weight 3. This representation is simple to understand and manipulate. The algorithm for working with edge lists involves: 1) Defining the graph as a list of edges. 2) Accessing elements: edge[0] gives source, edge[1] gives target, edge[2] gives weight. 3) Iterating through all edges to find relevant connections. Time complexity for lookup is O(E) since we might scan all edges.

connectsTo.reason: as the data structure that provides the edge weights for accumulation

commonMistakes: forgetting that nodes are often 1-indexed while arrays are 0-indexed
explanation.codePattern: graph = [(1,2,5), (2,3,3)]; for edge in graph: src, dst, w = edge[0], edge[1], edge[2]

examples.trace: Step 1: First edge [1,2,10]: from node 1 to node 2, weight 10. Step 2: Second edge [2,3,20]: from node 2 to node 3, weight 20. Step 3: No edges from node 3.

examples.output: Graph with 1 node: self-loop with weight 0
examples.trace: Single edge [1,1,0]: from node 1 to itself, weight 0.

approaches.name: Dijkstra with Binary Heap O(E log V)
approaches.summary: Use Dijkstra's algorithm with a binary min-heap to greedily select the node with the smallest known delay, updating distances to neighbors. This efficiently finds the shortest path delays in weighted graphs with non-negative edges.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Network Delay Time with Dijkstra's Binary Heap Implementation
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find the maximum shortest path delay from a source node to all other nodes in a weighted directed graph.

explanation.prose: This problem requires finding the shortest path delays from a source node to all other nodes in a weighted directed graph with non-negative edge weights, then returning the maximum of these delays. If any node is unreachable, return -1. Dijkstra's algorithm with a binary min-heap is optimal here because it efficiently processes nodes in order of increasing distance from the source. The intuition is greedy: always explore the node with the smallest known delay first, as non-negative weights ensure this path cannot be improved later. We maintain a min-heap of (delay, node) pairs, starting with (0, source). While the heap isn't empty, pop the smallest delay node. If we've already processed it with a smaller delay (using a visited set or distance comparison), skip it. Otherwise, mark it as processed and update its neighbors: for each neighbor, calculate new_delay = current_delay + edge_weight. If new_delay is smaller than the neighbor's known delay, update the distance array and push (new_delay, neighbor) to the heap. After processing, find the maximum distance in the array. If any distance remains infinity (unreachable), return -1.

timeComplexity.note: due to heap operations for each edge, and space is O(V + E) for adjacency list and heap

commonMistakes: using a max-heap instead of min-heap
or not skipping already-processed nodes in the heap (which can cause O(E log E) time). This directly implements the described approach
explanation.codePattern: adjacency list from times array, distances array initialized to infinity except source=0, heap = [(0, source)], while heap: pop (delay, node), if delay > distances[node]: continue, for neighbor, weight in graph[node]: new_delay = delay + weight, if new_delay < distances[neighbor]: update distances and push to heap, finally return max(distances) or -1 if any infinity

examples.trace: Step 1: Build adjacency list: {2: [(1,1), (3,1)], 3: [(4,1)], 1: [], 4: []}. Initialize distances: [inf, inf, 0, inf, inf] for nodes 1-4 (index 0 unused). Heap: [(0,2)]. Step 2: Pop (0,2). Delay=0, node=2. Update neighbors: neighbor=1, new_delay=0+1=1 < inf, so distances[1]=1, push (1,1); neighbor=3, new_delay=0+1=1 < inf, so distances[3]=1, push (1,3). Heap: [(1,1), (1,3)]. Step 3: Pop (1,1). Delay=1, node=1. No neighbors. Heap: [(1,3)]. Step 4: Pop (1,3). Delay=1, node=3. Update neighbor=4, new_delay=1+1=2 < inf, so distances[4]=2, push (2,4). Heap: [(2,4)]. Step 5: Pop (2,4). Delay=2, node=4. No neighbors. Heap empty. Distances: [inf, 1, 0, 1, 2]. Max=2, all reachable (no inf for nodes 1-4). Output: 2.

Step 1: Build adjacency list: {1: [(2,1)], 2: []}. Initialize distances: [inf, inf, 0] for nodes 1-2. Heap: [(0,2)]. Step 2: Pop (0,2). Delay=0, node=2. No neighbors. Heap empty. Distances: [inf, inf, 0]. Node 1 is inf (unreachable). Max would be inf, so return -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Dijkstra's Algorithm with Min-Heap for Single-Source Shortest Paths
ladders.desc: Implement Dijkstra's algorithm using a binary min-heap to compute shortest path distances from a source node.

explanation.prose: This sub-routine computes the shortest path distances from a source node to all other nodes in a weighted directed graph with non-negative edges, using Dijkstra's algorithm with a min-heap. The intuition is to greedily expand the node with the smallest known distance, as non-negative weights ensure this distance is final. We maintain a min-heap of (distance, node) pairs, initialized with (0, source). A distances array tracks the shortest known distance to each node, initialized to infinity except source=0. While the heap isn't empty, pop the smallest distance node. If its distance is greater than the stored distance (meaning it was updated later), skip it to avoid reprocessing. Otherwise, for each neighbor, calculate new_distance = current_distance + edge_weight. If new_distance is smaller than the neighbor's stored distance, update distances[neighbor] and push (new_distance, neighbor) to the heap. This ensures each node is processed once with its final distance.

timeComplexity.note: because each edge may cause a heap push, and heap operations are O(log V)

commonMistakes: not skipping outdated heap entries (leading to O(E log E) time) or using a list instead of a heap for O(V²) time. This feeds into L0 by providing the core distance computation; L0 adds the max-finding
explanation.codePattern: distances = [inf]*n, distances[source]=0, heap = [(0, source)], while heap: dist, node = heappop(heap), if dist > distances[node]: continue, for neighbor, weight in graph[node]: new_dist = dist + weight, if new_dist < distances[neighbor]: distances[neighbor] = new_dist, heappush(heap, (new_dist, neighbor))

examples.trace: Step 1: distances = [inf, 0, inf, inf] (index 0 unused). Heap: [(0,1)]. Step 2: Pop (0,1). dist=0, node=1. Update neighbors: neighbor=2, new_dist=0+1=1 < inf, so distances[2]=1, push (1,2); neighbor=3, new_dist=0+4=4 < inf, so distances[3]=4, push (4,3). Heap: [(1,2), (4,3)]. Step 3: Pop (1,2). dist=1, node=2. Update neighbor=3, new_dist=1+2=3 < 4, so distances[3]=3, push (3,3). Heap: [(3,3), (4,3)]. Step 4: Pop (3,3). dist=3, node=3. No neighbors. Heap: [(4,3)]. Step 5: Pop (4,3). dist=4 > distances[3]=3, so skip. Heap empty. Distances: [inf, 0, 1, 3]. Output: [0,1,3].

Step 1: distances = [inf, 0, inf]. Heap: [(0,1)]. Step 2: Pop (0,1). dist=0, node=1. Update neighbor=2, new_dist=0+5=5 < inf, so distances[2]=5, push (5,2). Heap: [(5,2)]. Step 3: Pop (5,2). dist=5, node=2. No neighbors. Heap empty. Distances: [inf, 0, 5]. Output: [0,5].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Node Selection with Min-Heap for Shortest Paths
ladders.desc: Use a min-heap to always process the node with the smallest known distance first in a graph traversal.

explanation.prose: This core logic involves using a binary min-heap to efficiently select the next node to process in Dijkstra's algorithm, based on the smallest known distance from the source. The intuition is greedy: by always expanding the node with the current minimum distance, we ensure that once a node is popped from the heap, its distance is final (due to non-negative edge weights). This works because any alternative path to that node would have to go through another node with a larger or equal distance, which cannot yield a smaller total distance. We maintain a heap of (distance, node) pairs. Initially, push (0, source). Repeatedly pop the smallest distance node. If its distance matches the stored shortest distance (in a distances array), process it; otherwise, skip it (as it's an outdated entry). When processing, for each neighbor, calculate potential new distance = current_distance + edge_weight. If this is less than the neighbor's stored distance, update the stored distance and push (new_distance, neighbor) to the heap. This ensures the heap always contains candidate distances for nodes, and we process nodes in increasing order of distance. Time complexity for heap operations is O(log V) per push/pop, leading to O(E log V) overall.

spaceComplexity.note: for the heap in worst case

commonMistakes: sorting (O(V log V) per pop) or not handling outdated heap entries. This feeds into L1 as the central mechanism of Dijkstra's algorithm
explanation.codePattern: heap = [], heappush(heap, (0, source)), while heap: dist, node = heappop(heap), if dist == distances[node]: process node and update neighbors

examples.input: distances = [inf, 0, inf, inf], graph = {1: [(2,1), (3,4)], 2: [(3,2)], 3: []}, heap initially [(0,1)]
examples.output: Process nodes 1,2,3 in order with updated distances
examples.trace: Step 1: Heap: [(0,1)]. Pop (0,1). dist=0 matches distances[1]=0, so process node 1. Update neighbors: for neighbor 2, new_dist=0+1=1 < inf, update distances[2]=1, push (1,2); for neighbor 3, new_dist=0+4=4 < inf, update distances[3]=4, push (4,3). Heap: [(1,2), (4,3)]. Step 2: Pop (1,2). dist=1 matches distances[2]=1, process node 2. Update neighbor 3: new_dist=1+2=3 < 4, update distances[3]=3, push (3,3). Heap: [(3,3), (4,3)]. Step 3: Pop (3,3). dist=3 matches distances[3]=3, process node 3. No neighbors. Heap: [(4,3)]. Step 4: Pop (4,3). dist=4 > distances[3]=3, so skip. Heap empty. Final distances: [inf, 0, 1, 3].

Step 1: Heap: [(0,1)]. Pop (0,1). dist=0 matches distances[1]=0, process node 1. Update neighbor 2: new_dist=0+5=5 < inf, update distances[2]=5, push (5,2). Heap: [(5,2)]. Step 2: Pop (5,2). dist=5 matches distances[2]=5, process node 2. No neighbors. Heap empty. Final distances: [inf, 0, 5].

ladders.title: Building Block — Binary Min-Heap Operations for Priority Queue
ladders.desc: Implement and use a binary min-heap to support insert and extract-min operations efficiently.

explanation.prose: A binary min-heap is a complete binary tree where each node's value is less than or equal to its children's values, enabling O(log n) insert and extract-min operations. This building block is crucial for Dijkstra's algorithm to manage nodes by priority (distance). The intuition is to maintain a dynamic set of items with priorities, always accessing the smallest quickly. Insertion: add the new element at the end, then 'bubble up' by swapping with its parent until the heap property is restored. Extract-min: remove the root (smallest), replace it with the last element, then 'bubble down' by swapping with the smallest child until restored. In practice, we use an array representation: for index i, parent at (i-1)//2, left child at 2*i+1, right at 2*i+2.

timeComplexity.note: for both operations, and space is O(n)

commonMistakes: incorrect index calculations or not handling heapify after operations. This feeds into L2 by providing the data structure for greedy selection

examples.trace: Step 1: Insert 3. Heap: [3]. Step 2: Insert 1. Heap: [3, 1]. Bubble up: compare 1 (index 1) with parent 3 (index 0). 1 < 3, so swap: heap becomes [1, 3]. Step 3: Insert 2. Heap: [1, 3, 2]. Bubble up: compare 2 (index 2) with parent 3 (index 0). 2 > 1? No, parent is 1 at index 0, actually parent index = (2-1)//2 = 0, value 1. 2 > 1, so no swap. Final heap: [1, 3, 2].

Step 1: Extract root 1. Heap: [1, 3, 2]. Replace root with last element: heap becomes [2, 3]. Remove last: heap size 2, [2, 3]. Bubble down: compare root 2 (index 0) with children: left child at index 1 is 3, right child none. 2 < 3, so no swap. Final heap: [2, 3]. Extracted value: 1.

testCases.expected: First extract: 1, heap [2,4,3]; second extract: 2, heap [3,4]

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Graph Representation as Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Convert a list of edges into an adjacency list for efficient graph traversal.

explanation.prose: An adjacency list represents a graph as a dictionary or array of lists, where each node points to a list of its neighbors along with edge weights. This basic operation is essential for Dijkstra's algorithm to quickly access neighbors. Given input as a list of edges [u, v, w] meaning directed edge from u to v with weight w, and n nodes, we build an adjacency list. The intuition is to store for each node, all outgoing edges so we can iterate over them in O(degree) time. We initialize an empty list for each node from 1 to n. For each edge, append (v, w) to the list for u. If the graph is undirected, do the same for both directions.

timeComplexity.note: to process all edges, and space is O(V + E) to store the lists

commonMistakes: or not handling missing nodes. This feeds into L3 by providing the graph structure for heap operations
explanation.codePattern: graph = {i: [] for i in range(1, n+1)} or graph = [[] for _ in range(n+1)], for u, v, w in edges: graph[u]

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize graph: {1: [], 2: [], 3: []}. Step 2: Process edge [1,2,1]: append (2,1) to graph[1] -> graph[1] = [(2,1)]. Step 3: Process edge [1,3,4]: append (3,4) to graph[1] -> graph[1] = [(2,1), (3,4)]. Step 4: Process edge [2,3,2]: append (3,2) to graph[2] -> graph[2] = [(3,2)]. Final: {1: [(2,1), (3,4)], 2: [(3,2)], 3: []}.
Step 1: Initialize graph: {1: [], 2: []}. Step 2: Process edge [2,1,1]: append (1,1) to graph[2] -> graph[2] = [(1,1)]. Final: {1: [], 2: [(1,1)]}.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graphs and Nodes with Weights
ladders.desc: Understand basic graph concepts: nodes, directed edges, and edge weights.

explanation.prose: A graph is a collection of nodes (vertices) and edges connecting them. In this context, we use a directed graph with weighted edges. Nodes represent entities (e.g., network devices), and directed edges represent one-way connections with weights indicating cost or delay (e.g., transmission time). For example, an edge from node A to node B with weight 5 means traveling from A to B takes 5 units. The foundation is to visualize or represent this structure: nodes are typically numbered 1 to n, and edges are given as triples (source, target, weight). Key intuition: graphs model relationships, and weights allow quantifying those relationships. This is prerequisite for all higher levels because Dijkstra's algorithm operates on such graphs.
commonMistakes: confusing directed vs undirected edges or misinterpreting weights. This feeds into L4 by providing the input format for adjacency list construction
explanation.codePattern: nodes = set of integers, edges = list of (u, v, w)

examples.trace: Step 1: Identify nodes: 1, 2, 3. Step 2: Edge 1->2 with weight 1: means from node 1 to node 2, cost 1. Step 3: Edge 2->3 with weight 2: means from node 2 to node 3, cost 2. Visual: 1 --1--> 2 --2--> 3. Representation: edges list = [(1,2,1), (2,3,2)].
Step 1: Nodes: 1, 2. Step 2: Edge 2->1 weight 3: from node 2 to node 1, cost 3. Visual: 2 --3--> 1. Representation: edges list = [(2,1,3)].

approaches.name: Bellman-Ford O(VE)
approaches.summary: Iteratively relax all edges V-1 times to compute shortest path delays, capable of handling graphs with negative weights. This approach is more general than Dijkstra but slower for typical non-negative cases.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Network Delay Time with Bellman-Ford
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find maximum shortest path delay from source to all nodes using Bellman-Ford algorithm

explanation.prose: The Bellman-Ford algorithm solves the single-source shortest path problem for graphs with potentially negative weights. For network delay time, we need to find the shortest path from a source node to all other nodes, then return the maximum of these distances. The intuition is that by relaxing all edges V-1 times (where V is number of vertices), we guarantee that shortest paths are found if they exist, since any simple shortest path can have at most V-1 edges. After V-1 iterations, we check for negative cycles by attempting one more relaxation - if any distance improves, a negative cycle exists. For network delay time, we assume no negative cycles (since delays can't be negative in reality), but the algorithm handles this case. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Initialize distances array with infinity for all nodes except source (0). 2) Repeat V-1 times: for each edge (u,v,w), if dist[u] + w < dist[v], update dist[v]. 3) Find maximum distance among reachable nodes. 4) Return -1 if any node remains unreachable (distance infinity).

connectsTo.reason: as the main algorithm uses the edge relaxation subroutine repeatedly

timeComplexity.note: where E is edges, space is O(V)

commonMistakes: forgetting to check for negative cycles in general shortest path problems
explanation.codePattern: distances = [float('inf')] * n; distances[source] = 0; for i in range(n-1): for u,v,w in edges: if distances[u] + w < distances[v]: distances[v] = distances[u] + w; max_dist = max(distances); return -1 if max_dist == float('inf') else max_dist

examples.trace: Initialize: distances = [inf, inf, 0, inf] (node 2 is source). Iteration 1: Edge (2,1,1): dist[2]+1=1 < inf → dist[1]=1. Edge (2,3,1): dist[2]+1=1 < inf → dist[3]=1. Edge (3,4,1): dist[3]+1=2 < inf → dist[4]=2. Iteration 2: Edge (2,1,1): dist[2]+1=1 = dist[1]=1 → no change. Edge (2,3,1): dist[2]+1=1 = dist[3]=1 → no change. Edge (3,4,1): dist[3]+1=2 = dist[4]=2 → no change. Iteration 3: (n-1=3 iterations total) all edges checked, no changes. Final distances: [inf, 1, 0, 1, 2]. Max distance among reachable nodes: max(1,0,1,2)=2. All nodes reachable? Node 0 (1-indexed node 1) is reachable (dist=1), so return 2.

Initialize: distances = [inf, inf] (node 2 is source, 1-indexed). Iteration 1: Edge (1,2,1): dist[1]+1=inf+1=inf → no change (can't relax from unreachable node). No more edges. After V-1=1 iteration: distances = [inf, 0]. Find max distance: max(inf,0)=inf. Since inf remains, node 1 is unreachable from source 2. Return -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Edge Relaxation in Bellman-Ford
ladders.desc: The core operation of checking and updating shortest path estimates through a single edge

explanation.prose: Edge relaxation is the fundamental operation in Bellman-Ford that progressively improves distance estimates. The intuition is simple: if we find a path to node v through node u that's shorter than our current best estimate to v, we update v's distance. Mathematically, for an edge from u to v with weight w, if dist[u] + w < dist[v], then dist[v] = dist[u] + w. This works because we're essentially testing whether going through u provides a better path to v. The step-by-step process: 1) Check if dist[u] is not infinity (meaning u is reachable). 2) Calculate candidate distance = dist[u] + w. 3) If candidate < dist[v], update dist[v] to candidate. Time complexity per relaxation is O(1), .
